reels.caption: 🇧🇷 Conor McGregor x Max Holloway não é apenas um duelo entre dois strikers de elite. A grande questão é: quanto uma paus
good: Hook text creates immediate conflict/curiosity with 'McGREGOR × HOLLOWAY' comparison frame
Bilingual captions (Portuguese + English) expand potential audience reach
Clear narrative hook: poses compelling question about comeback/time impact on fighters
Clean production quality and readable text overlays throughout
bad: Hook shows someone reading a document instead of McGregor or Holloway—title promises fight comparison but delivers third-party commentary, causing likely scroll-off
Hook is visually static (person sitting, reading)—no action or visual urgency to stop the scroll in first 1-3 seconds
No fight footage, B-roll, or dynamic visuals shown—just talking head close-ups, killing pacing for Reels format
Very low saves (2) despite 6k views suggests audience doesn't perceive this as valuable/rewatchable
reels.top_tip: Replace the hook entirely: open with actual McGregor or Holloway fight footage, a highlight clip, or a striking visual comparison—not someone reading. Viewers clicking for those fighters expect to see them immediately; deliver on the title promise in frame 1 or watch-time will tank further.

reels.caption:

#ufc329
good: High watch time (9.6s) shows viewers stay engaged with the full content arc
UFC branding and fighter names provide clear context and credibility
Live event authenticity and raw crowd perspective feel genuine and unpolished
bad: Phone operator/cameraman visible in background distracts from the action and looks amateurish
Hook leads with pre-fight staring instead of the actual KO moment — the title says 'KO's' but the title card doesn't show the knockout
Mid-video shows corner/ref work instead of action, breaking momentum
reels.top_tip: Open with the knockout blow itself (the actual finish) in the first 1 second instead of pre-fight staring — that's your scroll-stopping moment. Crop or remove the phone operator visible in frame.

reels.caption:
Luke Riley KO’s Kai Kamaka
#ufc329
good: Clear text overlay with fighter names immediately establishes the story
UFC branding visible—taps into established sport audience
Visible crowd reactions throughout add social proof of excitement
High reach-to-views ratio (78%) shows initial scroll-stop is working
bad: Hook shows buildup/context instead of the KO moment—viewers scroll before payoff arrives
Extremely low share rate (0.67%) and saves (0.14%) suggest content isn't shareable enough for an action KO
Caption promises KO but visual climax timing is unclear; dramatic moment may come too late
Only one hashtag (#ufc329)—severely limits discoverability compared to typical 8-15 tags for sports content
reels.top_tip: Move the KO moment to frames 1-3 seconds, then cut to crowd/aftermath reactions—put the climax first so viewers stay through completion, then reward them with context and hype

reels.caption: 🇺🇸 Knockouts create instant emotion. They require no context and no explanation. In an era dominated by TikTok, Instagra
good: Immediate hook: Bold red text overlay with fighter action creates instant visual impact and stops scrolls
Strategic escalation: Progresses from premise (strikers taking over) → emotional footage (KO/blood) → meta payoff, keeping attention
Dual language captions (EN/PT) expand reach and accessibility — smart for MMA's international audience
High engagement rate (15.8%) and 8.5s avg watch show content resonates; actual fight footage with impact is inherently shareable
bad: Critically low save count (1) relative to engagement — viewers consume but don't save for later; no CTA asking them to
Ending shifts from premise (fighter career implications) to dopamine/psychology commentary, which feels disconnected and may prompt premature scrolls
Share count (45) is decent but could be higher given the discussion angle — the video doesn't ask 'what's your take?'
No clear follow action — ending with a phone joke rather than reinforcing the take or directing to channel/subscribe
reels.top_tip: Add a mid-video CTA like 'Save this to settle the debate' or reframe the ending to reinforce the original question ('Is striking the future of MMA?') instead of pivoting to dopamine commentary — convert high engagement into saves and discussion

reels.caption:
Max Holloway vence Conor Mcgregor! Veja ai.
#ufc329
good: Clear hook text identifies the iconic Max Holloway vs Conor McGregor matchup instantly—viewers know exactly what fight they're watching
Solid viewer retention at 23.1s average watch time shows the fight footage itself is engaging enough to keep viewers watching
Professional UFC production quality with visible branding, sponsors, and octagon setup builds credibility
Live action in mid-video creates authenticity and intensity compared to edited highlights
bad: Caption spoils the result ('vence'/'wins') immediately, removing suspense and curiosity that drives sharing
Weak engagement trigger—'Veja ai' (check it out) doesn't prompt interaction; no question, poll, or debate angle
No visible dramatic peak in frames (knockout, submission, or celebratory moment) to create urgency or save-worthiness
Low share rate (6) and save rate (2) despite decent engagement indicate content isn't built for virality or discussion
reels.top_tip: Remove the result from caption and reframe as a guessing game: 'Who do you think won this UFC 329 classic?' + tag a friend—hiding the outcome and adding a direct engagement hook will convert passive viewers into active sharers and savers

good: Strong hook: clear knockout outcome + fighter names in text overlay grab attention immediately
